narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles from thousands of past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, test scores, and enrollment plans to forecast your bar exam success. For you specifically, it calculated a 51.6% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt—a threshold that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. This decision hinged on just a few standout factors from your profile that most strongly shaped the prediction. The most influential was your LSAT score of 36.50, which is very close to the average of 36.70 across applicants; this worked in your favor, nudging the model toward predicting you'd pass rather than fail. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the typical 5.53 average—pushed strongly in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to peers. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2, again lower than the average 5.71, reinforced concerns about early struggles that carried weight in the prediction. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.30, far below the 3.21 average, and your full-time enrollment status—which is common for 92.7% of students—played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers here. Overall, while your LSAT provided a boost, the model saw your lower decile rankings as red flags indicating challenges in sustaining strong school performance relative to typical admitted students, tipping the scales toward failure. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven risk assessment to protect the school's bar passage rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to strengthen that positive factor, improving your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and seeking targeted bar prep resources early—many students in similar positions have rebounded by addressing these exact areas before reapplying. With focused effort, you can shift your profile to align better with successful passers.
